thousands of patients.
Manufacturing process
controlled to minimize
impurities.14

potential for contamination with
host cell proteins (HCPs) and
aggregates, which can act as
adjuvants and increase
immunogenicity, exists without
any required testing.15

rows.based on data from\nthousands of patient:
Provided in a single-patient-
use, pre-filled autoinjector
pen designed and validated
for precise, fixed-dose
delivery.17
rows.potential for contamination with\nhost c:
Typically dispensed in multi-dose
vials requiring manual
measurement with a syringe, a
method the FDA has linked to
dosing errors and
hospitalizations.19

rows.based on data from\nthousands of patient:
The autoinjector’s fixed
needle length and automated
injection mechanism are
engineered to ensure
consistent subcutaneous (SC)
delivery.21
rows.potential for contamination with\nhost c:
Manual injection with a syringe
lacks controls for needle depth,
creating a significant risk of
inadvertent and improper
intramuscular (IM) or intradermal
(ID) administration, altering
pharmacokinetics and safety.22

rows.based on data from\nthousands of patient:
Mandatory post-market
adverse event (AE) reporting
by the manufacturer to the
FDA Adverse Event
Reporting System
(FAERS).25
rows.potential for contamination with\nhost c:
No federal requirement for 503A
pharmacies to report AEs to the
FDA, resulting in what the
Agency calls likely
“underreported” adverse events
and a critical public health blind
spot.19

rows.based on data from\nthousands of patient:
Risks are communicated
through FDA-approved
labeling and, when
necessary, through mandated
programs like Risk
Evaluation and Mitigation
Strategies (REMS) or DHCP
letters.26
rows.potential for contamination with\nhost c:
No requirement for compounders
to proactively inform prescribers
of the unique and significant risks
associated with their unapproved
products, creating a dangerous
information asymmetry.
